step_by_step_reasoning: We identify the system's key components based on the whiteboard and transcript: 1. Web/Mobile Users connect via ApiGateway. 2. ApiGateway routes traffic to AWS Lambda, which queries and updates a MongoDB database. 3. CloudWatch events trigger Lambda to export match data from MongoDB to S3. 4. External Game APIs feed live scores into Lambda, which saves them directly to S3. 5. Amazon EMR reads from S3 to compute leaderboard rankings. 6. Amazon RedShift queries S3 data to perform analytics for coupons and promotions.

graph.name: FanFight Daily Fantasy Sports Architecture
graph.notes: FanFight scales to support over 1.5M registered users with peak workloads during live sporting matches. The system ingests live game scores and user predictions, processes leaderboards via Spark on EMR, and stores metadata in MongoDB.

nodes.name: API Gateway
nodes.notes: Receives player requests and routes them to regional Lambdas.

nodes.name: AWS Lambda
nodes.notes: Orchestrates API requests, data ingestion, and S3 dumps.

nodes.name: MongoDB
nodes.notes: Primary transactional database for game state and users.

nodes.name: Amazon S3
nodes.notes: DATA_PEEK: Core data lake for raw game events and match metrics.

nodes.name: Amazon EMR
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: Runs Apache Spark jobs to compute rankings and winnings.

nodes.name: Amazon Redshift
nodes.notes: Performs user behavior analytics to optimize promotional campaigns.

edges.notes: Users query APIs to join contests and check status.
Exports match details to data lake.
Persists live points updates to data lake.
EMR pulls points data for processing.
Copies historical game data for analysis.
